item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as both images come from here and are owned by Gawker, thus making them non-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Ack. This one proved a little difficult to come up with a crosslink for, since its pretty standalone. Speaking with TwistedGears, the fact that this skip contains an interdimensional portal (as do many, many other anomalies, such as SCP-1322, or SCP-1763) was likely the best way to go. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

When a customer successfully boards, SCP-1741-B will autonomously travel down the river, disappearing from visual surveillance after approximately twenty meters of movement. Remote surveillance remains active, revealing the SCP-1741-B transports the customer to an archipelago of variable number1 of islands located outside of conventional space. Each island holds a designation of SCP-1741-#, and each exhibits a unique anomalous characteristic, available in the table below. The instance of SCP-1741-B will crash upon the shore of the newest island, and sink.

To this:

When a customer successfully boards, SCP-1741-B will autonomously travel down the river, disappearing from visual surveillance after approximately twenty meters of movement. Remote surveillance remains active, revealing the SCP-1741-B transports the customer to an archipelago of variable number2 of islands located outside of conventional space3. Each island holds a designation of SCP-1741-#, and each exhibits a unique anomalous characteristic, available in the table below. The instance of SCP-1741-B will crash upon the shore of the newest island, and sink.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Testing has revealed that the number of islands is dependent on the number of humans on the archipelago, currently four.
2. Testing has revealed that the number of islands is dependent on the number of humans on the archipelago, currently four.
3. Due to the suddenness of this transport, researchers have been unable to classify this spacetime anomaly, though similarity in activation to SCP-2539 suggests a it may be a variation of a Class-3 inter-dimensional portal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slow to start — it's initially just another generic abandoned theme park SCP — but it gets better, then pays off very well by the end. Also a really nice last line.

item.title: It's...
item.content:encoded:
…a video game, isn't it?? A virtual-reality video game. Tell me I'm at least close…

Either way, it's got an enthusiastic upvote from me. :-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spacetime shenanigans are going down. Wake Events are continuously triggering, causing a full reset of the archipelago. Which means D-17412, who died in the first log, is no longer dead. In the twelfth log he never turns the camera back on after meeting the tower god, so we miss him actually dying and why he was in those robes. D-17411 finds him dead, fills in the Foundation a bit, and then she escapes the archipelago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They also were heavily tied to African American and Chicano culture, so it's unlikely (although by no means impossible) that it would be the outfit of choice for a guy of European descent.

Nevertheless, the article is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only person confused about about how the first log features D-17412, who dies, and then the second log also starts with D-17412 alive, and then D-17412 is a corpse and D-17411 comes in out of nowhere? I suppose it could just be part of the whole space-time weirdness thing, but it seems more like maybe a typo than something with an underlying logic (even a weird space-time thing logic). That or, and there's certainly a good chance this is the case, I'm just missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice idea. I did prefer the interview over the description, otherwise this is a very well written piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. The description didn't really grab me, but the interview was really neat, and the glimpses from the islands cemented the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm, it appears that there's a pretty elaborate underlying story for this article, though I don't think that I grasp it fully.

Even though I basically have no idea what's actually going on, I still like this article a lot! It's got strong atmosphere and imagery, and it definitely leaves me wanting more information. Very nice work!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A minor nitpick here (seriously, minor, but it bugged me):

SCP-1741-C is a male humanoid of European descent, dressed in zoot suit.

And

Background study has revealed that Alan Pearce went missing in 1946.

Okay, so Alan Pearce (who 1741-C resembles) disappeared in 1946. While a zoot suit is from that era, it's unlikely he'd be wearing one. They were usually worn by younger men for nightclubbing, and were usually seen as extravagant and flashy - especially during the wartime, when there was fabric rationing.

So maybe it could be a plain men's suit in a dark grey? Or a trouser/sweater vest/shirt combination? Or, since it could be said he's a bit of a carny, plain brown trousers and a rumpled white shirt with folded up shirtsleeves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As stated before, I love this skip. I like that it leaves its employees "intact" and that the interviewed /wanted/ to tell us something. The archipelagos are brilliant as well. Well done TwistedGears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, you've explained this in chat now EXPLAIN IT HERE!

I really like this. I'd like to see an interview with the free Mr. Pearce, but that's probably taking away the mystery TELL US TELL US ALL RIGHT NOW!

item.title: Re: Author Notes
item.content:encoded:
Random note, don't know if it matters, but the originals are _probably_ from here:

http://www.urbanatrophy.com/locations/ef/ef.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As kindly confirmed by TwistedGears, I got this mostly right. The details of the archipelago are key.

I won't post spoilers because TwistedGears will cry. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this thing is probably ancient. Dates back to at least the 19th century, who knows how long before that. -C is the custodian, and the islands and puzzles are a test for the next one. Each island is probably tailored to the person—a constant war for the soldier, fruit of knowledge for the priest, who knows what the D-Class were but their islands probably also relate. After a person activates the Wake Event a certain number of times (10? The DECAlogue?), they become the new custodian. Wake Events reset everyone. There are mysterious "employers" testing the people for unknown reasons. The inside of -A is a fast-food-restaurant-equivalent based on the memories/experience of the current -C. There are mysterious employers testing these people for unknown reasons (entertainment?). Is ay of this even close to right?

EDIT: TwistedGears just explained everything to me in chat. I'm sort-of right! But not really.

item.title: Author Notes
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually not going to explain this one, at least right now. Let's see what people make of it.

Images are from the Visual Records, Abandoned tab. Unknown actual source.
